tools.description: Writes a short, tailored outbound email draft. Output is a draft — always review and personalize before sending.
tools.name: Prospect Email Draft
tools.system_prompt: Write a first-draft outbound email to {{prospect_name}} at {{company_name}}. Reference the context: {{context}}. Keep it under 120 words and professional.
tools.tagline: Draft a personalized outreach email from a few facts.

tools.description:
Produces a structured account briefing for a sales rep before a discovery call.

Covers company overview, likely pain points, and conversation hooks. Useful for Mid-Market and Enterprise segments.
tools.name: Account Research Brief
tools.system_prompt: You are a Navan revenue-operations research assistant. Produce a structured pre-call brief for {{company_name}} ({{company_website}}). Include: 1) company snapshot, 2) likely priorities in the {{segment}} segment, 3) three conversation openers, 4) risks or red flags. If any fact is unknown, write 'unknown' — never fabricate numbers or names.
tools.tagline: Generate a pre-call prospect profile in seconds.

tools.description: Analyzes usage, support, and engagement signals to surface churn risk level with a short rationale. Output drives review, not automated action.
tools.name: Churn Risk Check
tools.system_prompt: Evaluate churn risk for {{account_name}}. Usage trend: {{usage_trend}}. Last CSM touch: {{last_touch_days}} days ago. Support tickets open: {{open_tickets}}.
tools.tagline: Flag accounts showing early warning signs of churn.

tools.description: Given a company's basics, returns a structured ICP fit score with supporting reasons. Use as a starting point — always confirm with live data before routing.
tools.name: ICP Qualification Check
tools.system_prompt: Score ICP fit for {{company_name}} with {{employee_count}} employees in {{industry}} (HQ {{hq_location}}). Return a JSON object.
tools.tagline: Score how well an account fits Navan's ICP.

tools.description: Condenses notes, last touch, and deal stage into a tight call-prep summary. Treat as a starting point — the rep still needs to verify specifics in Salesforce.
tools.name: Call Prep Summary
tools.system_prompt: Produce a 2-minute call prep summary from the provided deal context: {{deal_name}} stage {{stage}}, last activity {{last_activity}}.
tools.tagline: Summarize open opportunities into a 2-minute prep sheet.

tools.app_html:
Meeting Prep Generator

Pre-call briefs backed by Account Research Brief

Recent preps

Generate a pre-call brief

We'll call the Account Research Brief tool and format the result for your meeting.
tools.description: One-page app for sales reps preparing for a call. Enter the company, contact, and meeting purpose; the app calls the Account Research Brief tool under the hood and formats the output into a pre-call brief (snapshot, priorities, openers, risks). The last five briefs are saved in the sidebar for quick recall and a print button produces a physical copy.
tools.name: Meeting Prep Generator
tools.tagline: Turn a company name into a structured pre-call brief in seconds.

tools.app_html:
Job Search Pipeline

0 applications tracked
N press N to add · drag cards between columns · click a card to edit

Add application
tools.description: Personal job-search kanban with six stages (Applied, Phone Screen, Interview, Final Round, Offer, Rejected). Add companies with role, date, salary range, and notes; drag cards between stages; press N to add a card. All data is saved in your personal app store via the Forge API.
tools.name: Job Search Pipeline
tools.tagline: Drag-and-drop kanban for tracking your job search end to end.

tools.description: Enter deals (company, stage, days in stage, value, close date) and the dashboard computes total pipeline value, average days in stage, at-risk deals (more than 30 days idle), and likely 30-day close value. Includes a Chart.js breakdown by stage and a one-click CSV export. Persists deals via the Forge app store.
tools.name: Pipeline Velocity Dashboard
tools.tagline: Manual pipeline tracker with velocity metrics and at-risk flags.

Produces a structured account briefing for a sales rep before a discovery call.

Covers company overview, likely pain points, and conversation hooks. Useful for Mid-Market and Enterprise segments.
response_preview.name: Account Research Brief
response_preview.system_prompt: You are a Navan revenue-operations research assistant. Produce a structured pre-call brief for {{company_name}} ({{company_website}}). Include: 1) company snapshot, 2) likely priorities in the {{segment}} segment, 3) three conversation openers, 4) risks or red flags. If any fact is unknown, write 'unknown' — never fabricate numbers or names.
response_preview.tagline: Generate a pre-call prospect profile in seconds.

skills.description: Drive a browser via Playwright to test UI end-to-end.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: webapp-testing
description: Test web applications via headless browser automation (Playwright): navigate, interact, assert visible state.
---

Use Playwright's Python or Node binding. Prefer role-based locators (getByRole) over brittle CSS selectors. Assert on rendered state, not implementation details.
skills.title: webapp-testing
skills.use_case: Verifying a web app actually works in a real browser

skills.description: Create a new skill correctly — frontmatter, trigger, structure.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: skill-creator
description: Create new skills with correct frontmatter (name, description), trigger phrasing, and directory layout.
---

A skill is a directory containing SKILL.md. Frontmatter must have 'name' and 'description'. The description is the trigger the model reads — make it describe when to invoke, not what the skill contains.
skills.title: skill-creator

skills.description: Scaffold a Model Context Protocol server the right way.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: mcp-builder
description: Build Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers with correct transport, tool registration, and schema definitions.
---

Start from the official SDK. Define tools with clear JSON schemas. Keep each tool focused on one action. Test locally with the MCP inspector before wiring into Claude.
skills.title: mcp-builder
skills.use_case: Building an MCP server to expose tools to Claude

skills.description: Build PowerPoint decks programmatically with layouts, images, and speaker notes.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: pptx
description: Create and edit Microsoft PowerPoint presentations, including layouts, text, images, tables, and speaker notes.
---

Use python-pptx. Pick a layout from the template, then populate placeholders by index. Add speaker notes via slide.notes_slide.notes_text_frame.
skills.title: pptx

skills.description: Create and edit Excel workbooks — formulas, formatting, multiple sheets.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: xlsx
description: Create and edit Microsoft Excel workbooks, including formulas, number/date formatting, styles, and multi-sheet workbooks.
---

Use openpyxl. Write formulas as strings beginning with '='. Preserve existing styles by reading them before overwriting cells.
skills.title: xlsx

skills.description: Create and edit Microsoft Word documents while preserving formatting.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: docx
description: Create, read, and edit Microsoft Word .docx files while preserving styles, headings, tables, and track changes.
---

Use python-docx. Preserve existing styles when inserting content. For track changes, operate on the underlying XML rather than the high-level API.
skills.title: docx

skills.description: Read, extract, create, and edit PDF files (forms, tables, text).
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: pdf
description: Comprehensive PDF processing including form filling, text/table extraction, creation from HTML, merging, splitting, and page manipulation.
---

Use pypdf / pdfplumber for reading. Use reportlab or HTML-to-PDF for generation. Fill forms by mapping field names to values. Extract tables with pdfplumber.
skills.title: pdf
skills.use_case: Anything involving PDFs — forms, extraction, generation

skills.description: Author new skills that actually get invoked at the right time.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: writing-skills
description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment.
---

A skill lives or dies by its description — that's the trigger the model sees. Write it so the model can tell, from the description alone, exactly when the skill applies. Test by asking an adjacent question and checking activation.
skills.title: writing-skills

skills.description: Fan out independent work to subagents when there's no shared state.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: dispatching-parallel-agents
description: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies.
---

Identify which tasks are truly independent. Dispatch each to its own subagent with a self-contained prompt. Merge results once all return.
skills.title: dispatching-parallel-agents
skills.use_case: Facing 2+ tasks with no sequential dependency

skills.description: Isolate feature work in a git worktree without disturbing the main checkout.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: using-git-worktrees
description: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - creates isolated git worktrees with smart directory selection and safety verification.
---

Create a worktree off the correct base branch. Verify clean state. Do the work. Return to the original checkout before cleaning up.
skills.title: using-git-worktrees
skills.use_case: Starting feature work or executing a plan in isolation

skills.description: Engage feedback with rigor, not performative agreement.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: receiving-code-review
description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation.
---

For each comment: restate what the reviewer is asking for, decide whether you agree, and if you disagree, push back with evidence. Do not blindly apply suggestions that would break correctness.
skills.title: receiving-code-review
skills.use_case: When responding to code review comments

skills.description: Ask for review so the reviewer can actually redirect you.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: requesting-code-review
description: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements.
---

State what the change does, why, and what would make it wrong. Point the reviewer at the riskiest diff and the test that should catch regressions.
skills.title: requesting-code-review
skills.use_case: Before merging a branch or completing a feature

skills.description: Execute a written plan in a separate session with review checkpoints.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: executing-plans
description: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints.
---

Work one plan step at a time. After each step, stop and summarize what changed, then wait for the human to redirect or greenlight the next step.
skills.title: executing-plans
skills.use_case: When you have a plan ready to implement

skills.description: Turn a spec into a reviewable, step-by-step implementation plan.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: writing-plans
description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code.
---

Break the work into verifiable steps. Each step states what changes, why, and how it will be tested. Surface risks up front. Write the plan so a reviewer can redirect cheaply before code is written.
skills.title: writing-plans
skills.use_case: Before touching code on a multi-step task

skills.description: Prove the work passes before claiming it does. Evidence first.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: verification-before-completion
description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always.
---

Run the tests. Run the linter. Run the type checker. Start the dev server and actually use the feature. Only then may you say the work is done.
skills.title: verification-before-completion
skills.use_case: Before committing, opening a PR, or claiming a task is done

skills.description: Hypothesis-driven root-cause investigation instead of guess-and-patch.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: systematic-debugging
description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes.
---

State the observed behavior, the expected behavior, and a hypothesis. Design the cheapest experiment that would falsify the hypothesis. Iterate until the root cause is proven, not just plausible.
skills.title: systematic-debugging
skills.use_case: When hitting any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior

skills.description: Red → Green → Refactor discipline for any feature or bugfix.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: test-driven-development
description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code.
---

Write the failing test first. Run it and confirm red. Write the minimum code to make it green. Refactor. Never write production code without a failing test.
skills.title: test-driven-development
skills.use_case: Before writing implementation code for a feature or bugfix

skills.description: Explore intent, requirements, and design before any creative work.
skills.prompt_text:
---
name: brainstorming
description: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
---

Use this skill to diverge before converging. Ask open questions about who the user is, what success looks like, and what constraints exist. Only move to implementation once the problem shape is clear.
skills.title: brainstorming
skills.use_case: Before creating features, components, or modifying behavior

response_preview.output:
# Pre-Call Brief: Acme Corp

## Company Snapshot

**Company Name:** Acme Corp  
**Website:** acme.example.com  
**Segment:** Mid-Market  
**Industry:** Unknown  
**Company Size:** Unknown  
**Key Business Focus:** Unknown  
**Recent News/Updates:** Unknown  

## Likely Priorities

- Optimizing operational efficiency and cost management across departments
- Improving visibility into spending and financial controls
- Streamlining expense management and reimbursement processes
- Enhancing compliance and audit readiness

## Conversation Openers

- "What visibility do you currently have into your team's spending across departments, and where are the biggest gaps?"
- "How are you managing expense policy compliance at scale, and what's creating the most friction for your finance team?"
- "What would it mean for your business if you could reduce the time spent on manual expense processing?"

## Risks / Red Flags

- Unknown decision-making structure and buying committee composition
- Unclear current technology stack and existing vendor relationships
- Unknown budget cycle and procurement timeline
- Potential resistance if expense management is not currently a stated priority
- Unknown competitive landscape and alternative solutions already in consideration
